skill.name: nextjs-best-practices
skill.description: Next.js App Router principles. Server Components, data fetching, routing patterns.
skill.summary: Essential patterns and best practices for building modern Next.js applications with the App Router architecture.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is documentation-only content describing Next.js App Router best practices. Static analysis flagged 18 patterns (external_commands, weak cryptographic algorithms), but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The SKILL.md file contains only educational markdown content with code examples - no executable code, no shell commands, no cryptographic operations. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Master Next.js App Router Best Practices
content.value_statement: Build production-ready Next.js applications with confidence. Learn Server Components, data fetching strategies, and routing patterns that scale.
actual_capabilities: Explain Server Component vs Client Component decision criteria
Provide data fetching patterns with proper caching strategies
Guide routing conventions including route groups and parallel routes
Recommend API route best practices with validation
Optimize performance with image and bundle strategies
Implement Server Actions for form mutations
limitations: Does not generate runnable code - provides guidance only
Focused on Next.js 13+ App Router, not Pages Router
Does not cover deployment or hosting configuration

use_cases.title: Migrating from Pages to App Router
use_cases.description: Transition existing Next.js projects to App Router architecture with proper Server Component patterns.
use_cases.target_user: Mid-level React developers upgrading legacy Next.js applications

use_cases.title: Building New Next.js Applications
use_cases.description: Start new projects with correct folder structure, caching strategies, and component architecture from day one.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developers creating production Next.js applications

use_cases.title: Code Review and Optimization
use_cases.description: Audit existing codebases for anti-patterns like unnecessary Client Components or missing loading states.
use_cases.target_user: Tech leads reviewing team code for Next.js best practices

prompt_templates.title: Component Type Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a component that displays user profile data and has a follow button. Should this be a Server Component or Client Component? Explain the reasoning.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - Understanding when to use each component type

prompt_templates.title: Data Fetching Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: What is the recommended fetch pattern for displaying product listings that should update every 5 minutes? Show the implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Route Organization
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I organize a dashboard with multiple sections (analytics, settings, users) that share a common layout but should not affect the URL structure?

prompt_templates.title: Full Feature Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a comment form using Server Actions. Include input validation with Zod, optimistic UI updates, and error handling. Show both the Server Component and Client Component code.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced - Combining Server Actions, validation, and Client interactivity

output_examples.input: Should I use useState in a Server Component?
output_examples.output: No. Server Components cannot use useState, useEffect, or event handlers. These hooks require browser JavaScript. If you need state, mark the component with 'use client' or extract the interactive parts into a separate Client Component.

output_examples.input: How do I cache API responses in Next.js?
output_examples.output: Use the fetch options: fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 } }) caches for 60 seconds. For static content, omit revalidate for build-time caching. Use 'no-store' to disable caching entirely.

output_examples.input: What is the purpose of loading.tsx?
output_examples.output: loading.tsx provides automatic loading states during navigation and data fetching. It renders while the new segment loads, improving perceived performance. Place it alongside page.tsx in any route segment.

best_practices: Start with Server Components by default, add Client Components only when interactivity is required
Use route groups with parentheses to organize routes without affecting the URL path
Implement loading.tsx and error.tsx for every major route segment to handle pending and error states
anti_patterns: Adding 'use client' to every component - this defeats the purpose of Server Components and increases bundle size
Fetching data in Client Components instead of Server Components - causes waterfall requests and slower loads
Skipping proper error boundaries - unhandled errors crash the entire application instead of showing fallback UI

faq.question: When should I use Server Components vs Client Components?
faq.answer: Use Server Components by default for data fetching, layout, and static content. Use Client Components only when you need useState, useEffect, event handlers, or browser APIs. Split large components to keep Client portions minimal.

faq.question: How does caching work in the App Router?
faq.answer: Next.js caches fetch requests by default. Use revalidate for time-based revalidation (ISR), revalidatePath or revalidateTag for on-demand updates, and no-store to disable caching. Each route segment can also have its own caching configuration.

faq.question: What are Server Actions and when should I use them?
faq.answer: Server Actions are async functions marked with 'use server' that run on the server. Use them for form submissions, data mutations, and triggering revalidation. Always validate inputs and handle errors properly.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in App Router?
faq.answer: Check authentication in Server Components before rendering protected content. Use middleware for route-level protection. For Client Components, fetch auth status from a Server Component or use a client-side auth provider.

faq.question: What is the difference between layout.tsx and template.tsx?
faq.answer: layout.tsx persists across navigation and maintains state. template.tsx creates a new instance on each navigation, remounting children. Use layout for persistent UI like navigation, template when you need reset behavior.

faq.question: Can I use API routes with the App Router?
faq.answer: Yes. Create route.ts files in the app directory with GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE handlers. Use Route Handlers for API endpoints. Consider Edge runtime for better performance on simple endpoints.
